claims.claim_text: CRDs are served and stored by the main Kubernetes API server, while aggregated APIs require programming and allow specialized API behavior such as custom storage.
claims.span_hint: section: CRDs vs API Aggregation; table + opening paragraphs
claims.rationale: The upstream custom-resources documentation explicitly contrasts CRDs with aggregated APIs and calls out custom storage and additional flexibility.

claims.claim_text: Upstream Kubernetes guidance warns against using custom resources as storage for application, end-user, or monitoring data.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters; paragraphs 1-3
claims.rationale: This is a direct recommendation in the custom-resources documentation and is central to the module's telemetry-vs-state framing.

claims.claim_text: The metrics APIs (`metrics.k8s.io`, `custom.metrics.k8s.io`, and `external.metrics.k8s.io`) are accessed through the aggregation layer; Metrics Server is the reference implementation for the resource Metrics API, and `kubectl top` fetches from Metrics Server.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters; later metrics example; section: Did You Know
claims.rationale: The HPA, resource metrics pipeline, and kubectl reference docs together directly back the aggregation-based metrics path and `kubectl top` behavior.

claims.claim_text: An `APIService` is named `version.group`, is HTTPS-only, uses `caBundle` to validate the backend serving certificate, strongly discourages `insecureSkipTLSVerify`, and references a Service that communicates on port 443.
claims.span_hint: section: The APIService Resource; opening paragraphs + YAML explanation
claims.rationale: These are explicit fields and semantics in the APIService reference.

claims.claim_text: `groupPriorityMinimum` determines API group preference during discovery, and `versionPriority` determines preferred version ordering within a group.
claims.span_hint: section: The APIService Resource; paragraph after APIService YAML
claims.rationale: The APIService reference defines both priority fields and their ordering behavior.

claims.claim_text: For aggregated API paths, kube-apiserver authenticates and authorizes the original request before proxying it to the extension apiserver.
claims.span_hint: section: The APIService Resource; request-path discussion around the first diagram
claims.rationale: The official aggregation-layer auth flow explicitly lists authentication and authorization on the main API server before proxying.

claims.claim_text: The original user identity is conveyed to the extension apiserver through headers such as `X-Remote-User` and `X-Remote-Group`, and header spoofing must be prevented by validating the authenticating proxy client certificate.
claims.span_hint: section: The APIService Resource; paragraphs after the APIService field table
claims.rationale: Both the auth reference and aggregation-layer configuration docs explicitly describe header-based identity and the client-certificate anti-spoofing requirement.

claims.claim_text: kube-apiserver creates the `extension-apiserver-authentication` ConfigMap in `kube-system`, and extension apiservers read it to validate the proxy CA, allowed names, and request-header names.
claims.span_hint: section: The APIService Resource; paragraph beginning "This trust model explains why..."
claims.rationale: The aggregation-layer configuration guide describes the ConfigMap contents and how extension apiservers use them.

claims.claim_text: Delegated authorization is commonly implemented with `SubjectAccessReview`, and extension apiservers typically need `system:auth-delegator` plus the `extension-apiserver-authentication-reader` role binding.
claims.span_hint: section: Deployment, TLS, and Delegated Authentication; RBAC paragraphs + bindings
claims.rationale: The official setup and aggregation-layer docs directly name the SAR flow and the standard RBAC roles involved.

claims.claim_text: Aggregated APIs must participate correctly in Kubernetes discovery, because clients learn supported groups, versions, resources, scope, and verbs through the Discovery API.
claims.span_hint: section: Building an Extension API Server; discovery paragraphs
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes API docs define the Discovery API as the mechanism clients use to learn group/version/resource metadata.

claims.claim_text: The aggregation layer runs in-process with kube-apiserver, and discovery requests to an extension API server must round-trip from kube-apiserver in five seconds or less.
claims.span_hint: section: Building an Extension API Server; discovery discussion; section: Did You Know
claims.rationale: The aggregation-layer concept page states both the in-process design and the five-second discovery round-trip requirement.

claims.claim_text: The specific diagnostic reason strings `ServiceNotFound` and `FailedDiscoveryCheck`, and the exact one-to-one mappings the module gives for them, need an authoritative source if kept verbatim.
claims.span_hint: section: Testing and Production Considerations; debugging paragraph after the first command block
claims.anchor_text: `ServiceNotFound` points to the `APIService` backend reference. `FailedDiscoveryCheck` usually means health, TLS, routing, or discovery JSON failed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Unavailable `APIService` conditions usually point first to backend Service references, TLS, health, routing, or discovery problems.
claims.rationale: The cited Kubernetes docs support the general troubleshooting layers, but not this exact pair of reason-string-to-cause mappings as written.

claims.claim_text: etcd recommends a default backend quota of 2 GiB and describes 8 GiB as a suggested maximum for normal environments.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know; etcd bullet
claims.rationale: The claim is real and primary, but the primary etcd documentation host is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The `cert-manager.io/inject-ca-from` annotation injects CA data into the `APIService` so the aggregator can validate the backend certificate.
claims.span_hint: section: Deployment, TLS, and Delegated Authentication; final paragraph before `manifests/apiservice.yaml`
claims.rationale: This is a cert-manager-specific behavior with an off-allowlist primary source.

claims.claim_text: The module's version-scoped wording about when the aggregation proxy is built into the main API server is more specific than the allowlisted source supports.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know; first bullet
claims.anchor_text: In Kubernetes 1.35 and later, the main API server binary includes the aggregation proxy and routes to registered `APIService` backends.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The main API server binary includes the aggregation proxy and routes requests to registered `APIService` backends.
claims.rationale: The official aggregation-layer doc supports the in-process proxy behavior, but not the specific `1.35 and later` cutoff.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes API Aggregation Layer
further_reading.why_relevant: Best high-level reference for what the aggregation layer is, how APIService registration works, and the discovery latency requirement.

further_reading.title: Configure the Aggregation Layer
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the real authn/authz flow, proxy client certificates, request headers, and delegated authorization.

further_reading.title: sample-apiserver
further_reading.why_relevant: Concrete upstream example of an extension apiserver implementation and manifests that match the aggregation model.
